data.title: Universal Music taps Hannah Poferl for top data role
data.description:
Universal Music Group on Tuesday named Hannah Poferl as chief data officer to oversee its global audience development and engagement strategy using data and artificial intelligence.

Poferl joins UMG after over a decade-long stint with the New York Times , where she has served as chief data officer since 2021. She will report to UMG's operating chief, Boyd Muir, effective immediately.

The move highlights the growing importance of data in the music industry, where streaming figures and social media metrics are increasingly leveraged to help artists and labels connect with fans.

"The organization's ambitious and thoughtful approach to innovation, including AI, reflects a clarity of purpose and a respect for creativity that I value deeply," Poferl said in a statement.

Her arrival comes as UMG doubles down on tech initiatives to stay competitive in the AI-driven music landscape. Earlier this month, the label partnered with Nvidia to use its AI infrastructure and train models for personalized recommendations.

In November, Bloomberg News reported that UMG, Sony Music and Warner Music licensed their catalogs to Klay, a music streaming startup that will let users remake songs using AI.

data.title: Fed officials cautious about rising productivity's contribution to inflation fight
data.description:
Key points:
  • Fed officials says it's too early to build productivity gains into interest rate outlook
  • Trump administration cites productivity gains in push for lower borrowing costs
  • US central bank not likely to cut rates soon

By Howard Schneider

Federal Reserve officials are hedging about how much rising productivity will help return inflation to the U.S. central bank's 2% target, reiterating this week they need to keep interest rates at current levels until it becomes clearer that price pressures will ease.

Higher productivity, all things equal, can let firms produce goods and services more cheaply, and recent strong productivity gains, along with hope for more to come from artificial intelligence investments, have prompted some in the Trump administration to argue the Fed should lower rates as a result.

But central bank officials said this week it was too early to build higher productivity into the outlook for rates, with monetary policy likely on hold for now in the face of inflation that is still running above target.

"I am hopeful that we are probably in a higher productivity regime. But I think it's too early to call that, and it's certainly too early to outsource our job of bringing inflation back towards 2%," St. Louis Fed President Alberto Musalem said in a webcast hosted by MNI. "I see little reason for near-term further easing of policy."

The Fed's current 3.50%-3.75% policy rate is roughly neutral, Musalem said, and doesn't need to fall unless it is clear that a currently "resilient" labor market is beginning to fail, or that inflation is falling faster than expected back to the central bank's target.

Underlying consumer inflation in December was 2.6% on a year-over-year basis, the same as in November, the Bureau of Labor Statistics reported on Tuesday, but food prices on a month-to-month basis jumped by the most in more than three years, and housing inflation was also strong.

President Donald Trump, however, said the new data showed there was "very low inflation" and should prompt the Fed to "cut interest rates, MEANINGFULLY."

The Fed is not expected to cut rates at its January 27-28 meeting, with many investors anticipating the central bank may remain on hold for the remainder of Fed Chair Jerome Powell's term as the head of its policymaking body, which ends in May after just three more meetings.

Trump has not named a successor, but tension has mounted between the two men after Powell disclosed in a statement on Sunday that he had been threatened with criminal indictment over testimony he gave to Congress last June.

COMPARISON TO GREENSPAN-ERA FED DEBATE

The policy debate, meanwhile, has continued in the background and begun to focus on productivity numbers that jumped 4.9% on a year-over-year basis in the most recent report, for the third quarter of last year, a number that drove down unit labor costs to nearly 2% on that same basis.

Trump administration officials, including top economic adviser Kevin Hassett, who is a candidate to succeed Powell, have said they are confident that trend will continue and, because it would help moderate inflation, provides a reason the Fed should reduce borrowing costs. Fed Governor Stephen Miran, on leave from his job as head of the White House's Council of Economic Advisers, has echoed that argument.

The U.S. central bank faced what some regard as an analogous moment in the mid-1990s, when then-Fed Chair Alan Greenspan rebuffed arguments by other officials for rate hikes at a moment when inflation pressure seemed to be building, anticipating that productivity would help inflation ease.

In comments on Monday, New York Fed President John Williams, an economist at the central bank's Washington-based Board of Governors in the 1990s, said he appreciated the parallels, but said he felt inflation was helped in that earlier episode not just by productivity, but by other factors, including expanded globalization, that are not currently present.

"I love positive shocks and supply shocks," Williams said at a Council on Foreign Relations event in New York, "but I think there were other factors that were helping keep inflation low" in the 1990s that are not the same today.

"I do not think the parallels are complete," said Williams, who aligned with Musalem in saying he did not see a reason to cut rates in the near term.

data.title: EIA Sees Lower Crude Oil Prices Slowing World Output Growth
data.description:
By Anthony Harrup

Growth in global crude oil production is likely to slow this year and in 2027 as a result of lower oil prices, leading to an eventual slowdown in inventory builds, the U.S. Energy Information Administration said Tuesday in its latest monthly outlook.

After estimated growth of 2.9 million barrels a day in 2025, global liquid fuels production is expected to increase by 1.4 million barrels a day this year and by 500,000 barrels a day in 2027, the EIA said. The agency sees oil consumption rising by 1.1 million barrels a day this year, down from 1.2 million barrels a day in 2025, and by 1.3 million barrels a day in 2027.

The EIA attributed the expected slowdown in production to lower oil prices. It estimates Brent crude will average $56 a barrel this year, down from $69 a barrel in 2025. U.S. benchmark West Texas Intermediate is expected to average $52 a barrel, down from $65 a barrel in 2025.

"We forecast that growing global oil production will continue to drive high global oil inventory builds," the EIA said. "However, inventory builds begin to gradually moderate next year, stemming price declines." The agency predicts inventory builds of 2.8 million barrels a day this year, similar to 2025, and 2.1 million barrels a day in 2027.

Signs of increasing volumes of oil in transit on water or in floating storage have started weighing on prices, and excess supply isn't yet reflected in observable inventories, the EIA said.

"We expect that lower near-term oil prices relative to those for oil deliveries further in the future (in other words, contango market structure) will encourage market participants to begin to store crude oil on land until supply and demand imbalances moderate and inventory builds decrease," the agency said.

Changing conditions in Venezuela are a key uncertainty in the forecast, the EIA said. "Any sanctions relaxation or other U.S. government policy changes related to Venezuela that could result in more oil production than we assumed in this forecast would put additional downward pressure on oil prices."

U.S. crude oil production is projected to remain around 13.6 million on average in 2026 before dropping to 13.3 million barrels a day in 2027 as sustained lower oil prices lead to a decline in drilling and well completion activity that outweigh increases in productivity.

A decline in output this year in the Lower 48 states is seen largely offset by higher offshore production in the Gulf and increased output in Alaska, the EIA said.

Write to Anthony Harrup at anthony.harrup@wsj.com

data.title: US FDA extends review of Travere’s drug for rare kidney disease
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has extended its review for the expanded use of Travere Therapeutics’ drug to treat a type of rare kidney disorder, the drugmaker said on Tuesday.

The agency will now give its decision on April 13, compared to its previous action date of January 13.

Travere's drug, Sparsentan, was being tested in patients with focal segmental glomerulosclerosis (FSGS), a kidney disease that scars parts of the filtering units, leading to protein in the urine, swelling and possible kidney failure.

Sparsentan, under the brand name, Filspari, is already approved to slow kidney function decline in adults with IgA nephropathy, a progressive autoimmune condition.

data.title: Thirty-Year Bond Auction Attracts Above Average Demand
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - A day after revealing this month's three-year and ten-year note auctions attracted average demand, the Treasury Department revealed this month's auction of $22 billion worth of thirty-year bonds attracted above average demand.

The thirty-year bond auction drew a high yield of 4.825 percent and a bid-to-cover ratio of 2.42.

Last month, the Treasury also sold $22 billion worth of thirty-year bonds, drawing a high yield of 4.773 percent and a bid-to-cover ratio of 2.36.

The bid-to-cover ratio is a measure of demand that indicates the amount of bids for each dollar worth of securities being sold.

The ten previous thirty-year bond auctions had an average bid-to-cover ratio of 2.36.

data.title: Rio Tinto engages three banks to advise on potential Glencore acquisition, source says
data.description:
By Andres Gonzalez

Rio Tinto , has engaged JPMorgan and two other advisers on its potential acquisition of Glencore , a deal that could create the world's largest miner worth over $200 billion, a source with knowledge of the situation told Reuters.

The other banks Rio Tinto has engaged are Evercore and Australian financial services group Macquarie, the person said. The roles are highly prized as bankers jostle for a share of potentially more than $100 million in advisory fees that such a deal could generate.

The potential transaction represents the latest attempt at consolidation in the global mining industry, as companies race to secure reserves of metals including copper, needed for the energy transition and artificial intelligence.

Glencore has not yet formally retained an adviser, a second person with knowledge of the matter said. The people spoke on condition of anonymity because the matter is private. Citi retains ties to the company as the U.S. bank has advised it on previous deals including its failed acquisition of Teck in 2023, Reuters reported previously.

The two mining giants have explored combining operations before, with Rio Tinto rejecting a Glencore merger offer in 2014, saying it was not in shareholders' interest, while more recent merger talks in late 2024 also concluded without an agreement.

Bloomberg previously reported the names of banks advising on the latest attempt.

data.title: These surprising states rank first and worst for retirees
data.description:
By Jessica Hall

Healthcare access, taxes and affordability help dictate the best places to retire

Wyoming got top marks in key areas such as taxes, affordability and the health of its residents.

The best place to retire isn't necessarily the one that has 350 days of sun, the best pickleball courts and in-ground swimming pools. It may instead be where you pay the lowest taxes and where people have fewer chronic medical conditions, new research argues.

As people live longer, where to retire takes on more significance. Location can greatly influence healthcare outcomes, the affordability of everyday goods and services, and quality of life.

With that in mind, Wyoming ranked as the best place to retire in 2026 due to its lack of personal income tax and the nation's lowest rate of multiple chronic conditions among Medicare beneficiaries, according to a report by CareScout, a company that helps families find aging care.

Home of Yellowstone and Grand Teton national parks, Wyoming has a moderate cost of living and solid average Social Security income of $28,082, the report found. The state ranked No. 3 on CareScout's list last year.

"The affordability piece rockets Wyoming up to the top, along with tax friendliness," said Marlena del Hierro, CareScout's director of operational growth.

While Wyoming has certain financial and outdoor benefits to enjoy, there are trade-offs in other areas, such as healthcare-provider availability. The rate of doctors per 10,000 residents is lower in Wyoming, del Hierro noted, though technology such as telehealth may help fill in gaps if there is not a physician nearby.

The report comes as roughly 18% of the U.S. population is now age 65 or older. An adult turning 65 today can expect to live nearly two more decades, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, which puts increasing importance on where to retire since retirement itself could last decades.

More than 1 in 3 retirees have moved during retirement, according to research by the nonprofit Transamerica Institute and its Transamerica Center for Retirement Studies. Among those who moved, the top reasons included moving closer to family and friends, downsizing into a smaller home, starting a new chapter in life, reducing expenses, moving to a better climate and no longer needing to live near work, Transamerica found.

Joseph Coughlin, director of the Massachusetts Institute of Technology's AgeLab and a MarketWatch contributor, found that retirees may move as many as five times in retirement for reasons including downsizing and potentially entering assisted-living or skilled-nursing facilities.

In 2025, other rankings have highlighted New Hampshire and Midland, Mich., as the best places to retire. New Hampshire nabbed the top spot from Bankrate due to high scores in neighborhood safety, low local taxes, quality healthcare, the number of people of a similar age, affordability, and access to arts and entertainment. Meanwhile, Midland, Mich. got top marks from U.S. News & World Report for its quality of life, affordability and retiree taxes.

For the CareScout ranking, other states in the top five included New Hampshire, Vermont, Montana and South Dakota. Like Wyoming, New Hampshire (No. 2) has no personal income tax; it also had the second-highest Social Security income in the U.S., at $29,422. Vermont (No. 3) stood out with one of the country's highest proportions of older adults, at 22.9% of its population, CareScout said.

Montana (No. 4) has 687 arts and recreational venues per 100,000 older adults, while South Dakota (No. 5) combined affordability with no income tax and strong recreational opportunities, CareScout said.

On the other end of the spectrum, New Jersey came in last, ranking 51st in CareScout's tally of U.S. states and the District of Columbia. New Jersey has the highest average Social Security income of $29,562, but also has a high cost of living and top personal income-tax rate, as well as poor aging health overall - 68% of Medicare beneficiaries have three or more chronic conditions, CareScout found. This is the second year in a row that New Jersey came in last on the list.

Other states at the bottom of the rankings included Massachusetts, New York, Alabama and Mississippi. Massachusetts (No. 50) and New York (No. 49) both incurred high costs of living and high personal income tax burdens. That was offset by strong doctor availability.

Meanwhile, Alabama (No. 48) and Mississippi (No. 47) have low costs of living but grapple with some of the worst rankings in aging health - with 71% and 67% of Medicare beneficiaries, respectively, having three or more chronic conditions. The states also have poor access to arts and recreational centers.

Well-known retirement hotspots such as Florida and Arizona ranked No. 7 and No. 22, respectively, according to CareScout.

"Once you're not tied down with an employer, where do you want to live opens up a whole new world of possibilities," said Catherine Collinson, CEO and president of Transamerica Institute and Transamerica Center for Retirement Studies. "I actually love reading the lists of the 'best' and 'worst.' I love thinking about new possibilities and imagining what a new chapter would look like."

Collinson urged people considering relocating in retirement to first rent temporarily in the new locale, to take the location on a test drive before moving to make sure it meets their expectations.

"Decisions to move in retirement - the where, the when, the how - are so intensely personal," Collinson said.

There's no shortage of rankings of the best and worst places to retire. While some other listings weigh weather, taxes and leisure more heavily, the CareScout analysis looked at nine categories of data in all 50 states and D.C. across three main categories - affordability, healthcare and quality of life - from sources including the Social Security Administration, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, the Bureau of Labor Statistics, the U.S. Census Bureau, the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration and more.

"This ranking can help people project and plan where they may want to be," del Hierro said. "You have to think about: Where is your community? Does that push you to relocate? When you're retired, you have another chapter to live, so it helps to plan in advance."

-Jessica Hall

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Trump says USMCA is irrelevant for US
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Tuesday said the United States-Mexico-Canada trade agreement (USMCA) is not relevant for the U.S. but Canada wants it, as he pushed for companies to bring manufacturing back to American soil.

"There's no real advantage to it, it's irrelevant," Trump said. "Canada would love it. Canada wants it. They need it."

Trump made the comments as he toured a Ford Motor Co. factory in Dearborn, Michigan, ahead of a speech on the economy he was scheduled to deliver in Detroit.

"The problem is we don't need their product. You know, we don't need cars made in Canada. We don't need cars made in Mexico. We want to take them here. And that's what's happening," he said.

The USMCA is up for review this year to decide whether it will be left to expire or another deal will be worked out.

The trade pact, which replaced the North American Free Trade Agreement in 2020 and was negotiated during Trump's first term as president, requires the three countries to hold a joint review after six years.

data.title: Nymex Overview: Oil Futures Rise as Geopolitical Tensions Increase Worries over Supply — OPIS
data.description:
Oil futures were trading at seven-week high early Tuesday afternoon on continued geopolitical concerns that traders worry could led to cuts in output.

The NYMEX February West Texas Intermediate contract was up $1.50 to $61.50/bbl at about 12:40 p.m. ET and the March WTI contract was $1.42 higher at $60.74/bbl.

The ICE Brent March contract was up $1.43 to $65.30/bbl and the April contract was $1.37 higher at $64.59/bbl.

Refined products were also posting solid gains. The NYMEX February RBOB contract was 3.03cts higher at $1.8241/gal and the March contract was up 3.08cts to $1.8528/gal. The February ULSD contract was up 7.55cts to $2.2299/gal and March was 6.92cts higher at $2.2175/gal.

The fallout from Iran's antigovernment demonstrations, including government crackdowns and escalating unrest, has heightened concerns over potential disruptions to production in the country. President Trump's public encouragement of protesters and his threat to impose 25% tariffs on countries trading with Iran have reinforced market risk sentiment, particularly given China's role as the main importer of Iranian crude.

U.S. intervention in Venezuela has opened the door for more oil from that country to reach the market. But questions about Iranian production and potential disruptions of exports from Kazakhstan have raised supply worries.

Some technical factors, including the unwinding of short positions, have also supported price momentum.

A series of disruptions at the Caspian Pipeline Consortium export terminal on Russia's Black Sea coast, including recent drone strikes and earlier weather and maintenance issues, has significantly constrained Kazakh export volumes.

Because the consortium accounts for the bulk of Kazakhstan's crude exports, the bottleneck is limiting flows and adding to supply concerns.

This content was created by Oil Price Information Service, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. OPIS is run independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

By Allegra Fradkin, afradkin@opisnet.com; Editing by Jeffrey Barber, jbarber@opisnet.com

data.title: Delta CEO Says World Cup Could Break Inbound Travel 'Logjam' — Market Talk
data.description: The World Cup being largely hosted in the U.S. this year can hopefully break the "international logjam" of inbound travel that's been a pain point for domestic carriers over the past year, Delta CEO Ed Bastian says on a call with reporters. "You'll have hundreds of thousands of international visitors, many of whom may be, for the first time, coming to the U.S.," Bastian says. The slowdown in travel to the U.S. has been attributed by analysts to the Trump administration's immigration policies, trade disputes and divisive political rhetoric, along with a relatively strong dollar that has made tourism in the U.S. more expensive. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

data.title: US earnings season off to a mixed start ​​
data.description:
Key points:
  • Nasdaq edges red, S&P 500 dips, Dow declines ~0.75%
  • Financials weakest S&P 500 sector; Energy leads gainers
  • Dollar gains; bitcoin, crude both rally >2.5%; gold slips
  • US 10-Year Treasury yield edges down to ~4.17%

US EARNINGS SEASON OFF TO A MIXED START

The latest earnings season got off to a somewhat mixed start on Tuesday, with shares of JPMorgan Chase down more than 3.5% in early afternoon trading even as it reported a quarterly profit that exceeded expectations.

Shares of BNY , which reported a rise in fourth-quarter profit and raised the target for a key profitability measure, are higher by about 0.5%.

At the same time, shares of Delta Air Lines are down just over 2% after the mid-point of the carrier's 2026 profit forecast fell short of analysts' expectations. This is dragging down other airline shares .

Other financials are mostly lower, with the S&P 500 financial index down 1.7% and the S&P 500 banks index (.SPXBK) down 2%. Financials is the weakest S&P 500 sector on the day.

Citigroup , Bank of America and Wells Fargo are all lower, and are due to report quarterly results on Wednesday.

Heading into the earnings season, earnings growth for Q4 is estimated to be down from the previous quarter.

Analysts saw earnings growth for the fourth quarter for S&P 500 companies overall of 8.8% year-over-year, down from 14.9% in the third quarter, according to LSEG data as of Friday. Earnings growth for the financial sector was seen at 6.8%, down from 25% in the third quarter of 2025, based on the data.
